title: Mental Health — Delirium & Dementia (Set 7)
subtitle: Neurocognitive disorders: delirium vs dementia, safety, communication & behavior
scenario: Caring for clients with cognitive impairment: distinguishing delirium from dementia, ensuring safety, and using supportive communication and behavioral approaches.

steps.q: Delirium is distinguished from dementia primarily by its:

opts.text: Acute onset with a fluctuating, often reversible course
opts.rat: Delirium develops rapidly, fluctuates, and is frequently reversible once the underlying cause is treated, unlike the gradual, progressive course of dementia.

opts.text: Gradual, irreversible decline over years
opts.rat: That describes dementia, not delirium.

opts.text: Complete absence of any medical cause
opts.rat: Delirium usually has an identifiable medical cause.

opts.text: Occurrence only in young adults
opts.rat: Delirium is common in older and medically ill clients, not limited to young adults.

steps.q: An older adult on the unit becomes suddenly confused and agitated overnight after being clear earlier in the day. The technician should FIRST suspect:

opts.text: Normal aging
opts.rat: Sudden confusion is not a feature of normal aging.

opts.text: A possible acute medical cause such as infection (e.g., UTI), medication, or dehydration
opts.rat: Acute, sudden confusion (delirium) in an older adult often signals an underlying medical problem and requires assessment and reporting.

opts.text: Attention-seeking behavior
opts.rat: Labeling delirium as attention-seeking risks missing a medical emergency.

opts.text: Permanent worsening of dementia that cannot be treated
opts.rat: A sudden change suggests a possibly reversible cause, not simply irreversible decline.

steps.q: The PRIORITY in caring for a client with delirium is to:

opts.text: Accept it as an untreatable part of dementia
opts.rat: Delirium is often reversible and should not be dismissed.

opts.text: Restrain the client until it resolves on its own
opts.rat: Restraint does not treat the cause and can worsen agitation.

opts.text: Identify and treat the underlying cause while keeping the client safe
opts.rat: Because delirium is usually caused by a treatable medical condition, finding and correcting the cause—while ensuring safety—is the priority.

opts.text: Withhold all fluids
opts.rat: Dehydration can cause or worsen delirium; withholding fluids is harmful.

steps.q: Dementia (such as Alzheimer disease) is BEST described as:

opts.text: A sudden, temporary confusion that resolves in hours
opts.rat: That describes delirium, not dementia.

opts.text: A mood disorder with periods of mania
opts.rat: That describes bipolar disorder.

opts.text: A normal, expected part of aging
opts.rat: Dementia is a disease process, not normal aging.

opts.text: A gradual, progressive, usually irreversible decline in memory and cognition
opts.rat: Dementia involves a slow, progressive, generally irreversible decline in memory and other cognitive functions.

steps.q: A client with dementia becomes increasingly confused and agitated in the late afternoon and evening. This pattern is known as:

opts.text: Sundowning
opts.rat: Sundowning is increased confusion and agitation in the late afternoon/evening; a calm environment, adequate lighting, and routine help manage it.

opts.text: Confabulation
opts.rat: Confabulation is filling memory gaps with invented information.

opts.text: Perseveration
opts.rat: Perseveration is the repetition of words or actions.

opts.text: Aphasia
opts.rat: Aphasia is impaired language ability.

steps.q: The FIRST-line approach to behavioral symptoms of dementia (such as agitation) is:

opts.text: Immediate physical restraint
opts.rat: Restraint is a last resort and often worsens agitation and injury risk.

opts.text: Nonpharmacologic measures such as redirection, routine, and a calm environment
opts.rat: Nonpharmacologic strategies—redirection, consistent routine, calm surroundings, meaningful activity—are first-line for behavioral symptoms of dementia.

opts.text: Arguing with the client to correct their confusion
opts.rat: Arguing increases agitation and distress.

opts.text: Frequent changes of caregiver and room
opts.rat: Changes and unfamiliarity increase confusion and agitation.

steps.q: When communicating with a client who has moderate dementia, the technician should:

opts.text: Give several complex instructions at once
opts.rat: Multiple or complex instructions overwhelm a client with dementia.

opts.text: Speak rapidly from behind the client
opts.rat: Approaching from behind and speaking quickly can startle and confuse the client.

opts.text: Use short, simple sentences and one instruction at a time, facing the client calmly
opts.rat: Simple, one-step directions delivered calmly and face-to-face, with time to respond, support understanding in dementia.

opts.text: Quiz the client repeatedly on the date and year
opts.rat: Repeated quizzing can increase frustration and distress.

steps.q: A client with dementia insists on 'going home to make dinner for the children,' though her children are adults. The BEST response is to:

opts.text: Bluntly tell her the children are grown and she is confused
opts.rat: Confronting the client with reality can cause distress and agitation.

opts.text: Argue until she agrees she is wrong
opts.rat: Arguing increases agitation and does not help.

opts.text: Ignore her and walk away
opts.rat: Ignoring the client does not address her distress.

opts.text: Acknowledge her feelings and gently redirect her to a calming activity
opts.rat: Validating the client's feelings and redirecting to a soothing activity is more therapeutic than confrontation in dementia care.

steps.q: A client with dementia tends to wander. The BEST safety measure is to:

opts.text: Provide a safe area to walk and use appropriate monitoring/alarms per policy
opts.rat: Allowing safe wandering with monitoring meets the client's need to move while preventing harm, which is preferable to restraint.

opts.text: Keep the client restrained in a chair all day
opts.rat: Restraint increases agitation, deconditioning, and injury risk.

opts.text: Leave exit doors unmonitored
opts.rat: Unmonitored exits allow elopement and danger.

opts.text: Sedate the client to prevent movement
opts.rat: Sedation for staff convenience is inappropriate and increases fall risk.

steps.q: Which environment BEST supports a client with dementia?

opts.text: Frequently rearranged rooms with new staff each day
opts.rat: Change and unfamiliarity increase confusion for a client with dementia.

opts.text: A consistent routine, familiar surroundings, and calm, low stimulation
opts.rat: Consistency, familiarity, and a calm environment reduce confusion and agitation in dementia.

opts.text: Loud, busy areas to keep the client stimulated
opts.rat: Overstimulation increases agitation.

opts.text: Dim, cluttered spaces
opts.rat: Poor lighting and clutter increase confusion and fall risk.

steps.q: In the early stage of Alzheimer disease, which type of memory is typically affected FIRST?

opts.text: Memories from childhood
opts.rat: Remote/long-term memories are usually retained longer than recent memory.

opts.text: The ability to speak any words at all
opts.rat: Total loss of speech occurs in advanced, not early, disease.

opts.text: Recent (short-term) memory
opts.rat: Early Alzheimer disease characteristically impairs recent/short-term memory first, while remote memories are relatively preserved until later.

opts.text: No memory is affected in the early stage
opts.rat: Recent memory loss is an early hallmark of Alzheimer disease.

steps.q: For a client with advanced dementia who is distressed, 'validation therapy' means the technician:

opts.text: Repeatedly corrects the client with the true date and facts
opts.rat: That describes reality orientation, which can distress a client with advanced dementia.

opts.text: Ignores the client's emotions
opts.rat: Ignoring emotions is not therapeutic.

opts.text: Argues until the client accepts reality
opts.rat: Arguing increases agitation.

opts.text: Acknowledges and responds to the client's underlying feelings and reality
opts.rat: Validation therapy meets the client in their own reality and responds to feelings, reducing distress in advanced dementia.

steps.q: A client with dementia becomes overwhelmed and has a sudden emotional outburst (catastrophic reaction). The technician should:

opts.text: Reduce stimulation, remain calm, and provide reassurance
opts.rat: A catastrophic reaction is an overreaction to overwhelming stimuli; lowering stimulation and staying calm help the client settle.

opts.text: Raise your voice to get the client's attention
opts.rat: Raising your voice increases the client's distress.

opts.text: Add more activity and noise to distract them
opts.rat: More stimulation worsens a catastrophic reaction.

opts.text: Immediately apply restraints
opts.rat: Restraint escalates distress and is not first-line.

steps.q: To support activities of daily living for a client with moderate dementia, the technician should:

opts.text: Do everything for the client to save time
opts.rat: Doing everything promotes dependence and loss of function.

opts.text: Break tasks into simple steps, give cues, and allow the client to do what they can
opts.rat: Simple step-by-step cueing that preserves the client's remaining abilities supports dignity and function.

opts.text: Rush the client to finish quickly
opts.rat: Rushing increases frustration and failure.

opts.text: Withhold assistance entirely
opts.rat: Some cueing and support are needed; total withholding is inappropriate.

steps.q: A client with dementia is eating poorly and becomes distracted at meals. A helpful intervention is to:

opts.text: Serve meals in a loud, crowded room
opts.rat: Noise and crowding increase distraction and reduce intake.

opts.text: Present a complicated tray with many utensils
opts.rat: Complex place settings confuse and overwhelm the client.

opts.text: Offer easy-to-handle finger foods and reduce mealtime distractions
opts.rat: Finger foods and a calm, low-distraction setting support nutrition and independence for a client with dementia.

opts.text: Withhold food if the client does not eat quickly
opts.rat: Withholding food is harmful and worsens nutrition.

steps.q: Why are physical restraints generally AVOIDED for confused older clients with dementia?

opts.text: They calm the client and prevent all injury
opts.rat: Restraints do not calm clients; they often increase agitation.

opts.text: They improve the client's memory
opts.rat: Restraints have no benefit to memory.

opts.text: They are required for all dementia clients
opts.rat: Restraints are a last resort, never routine.

opts.text: They can increase agitation, injury, and deconditioning
opts.rat: Restraints tend to worsen agitation and lead to injury, immobility, and deconditioning; least-restrictive alternatives are preferred.

steps.q: A nonverbal client with advanced dementia suddenly becomes agitated and strikes out during care. The technician should FIRST:

opts.text: Assess for an unmet need such as pain, hunger, or need to toilet
opts.rat: In advanced dementia, new agitation often signals an unmet need or discomfort; assessing for pain, hunger, or toileting is the first step.

opts.text: Assume the client is simply being difficult
opts.rat: Dismissing behavior ignores a likely unmet need.

opts.text: Immediately medicate for aggression
opts.rat: Medication is not the first response before assessing for a cause.

opts.text: Continue the care task quickly despite the agitation
opts.rat: Forcing care during agitation increases distress and risk.

steps.q: When giving medications to an older adult with cognitive impairment, the technician recognizes that certain drugs can worsen confusion, especially:

opts.text: Standard multivitamins
opts.rat: Routine vitamins do not typically worsen confusion.

opts.text: Anticholinergic and sedative medications
opts.rat: Anticholinergics and sedatives (including some sleep aids) can worsen confusion in older adults; the principle is 'start low, go slow.'

opts.text: Artificial tears
opts.rat: Eye drops do not cause confusion.

opts.text: Topical skin moisturizer
opts.rat: Moisturizer has no effect on cognition.

steps.q: A client with dementia repeatedly asks the same question every few minutes. The BEST technician response is to:

opts.text: Tell the client you already answered and to stop asking
opts.rat: This is frustrating and unhelpful for a client who cannot remember.

opts.text: Ignore the client completely
opts.rat: Ignoring the client is dismissive.

opts.text: Answer calmly and simply each time, and try gentle redirection
opts.rat: Because the client cannot retain the answer, responding calmly and redirecting is more therapeutic than showing frustration.

opts.text: Quiz the client on why they keep forgetting
opts.rat: Quizzing increases distress and does not help.

steps.q: The family caregiver of a client with dementia appears exhausted and overwhelmed. A supportive technician action is to:

opts.text: Tell the caregiver they should simply try harder
opts.rat: This is dismissive and adds guilt.

opts.text: Advise the caregiver to stop visiting
opts.rat: Discouraging family involvement is not supportive.

opts.text: Ignore the caregiver's distress
opts.rat: Caregiver well-being affects the client and should not be ignored.

opts.text: Acknowledge the caregiver's stress and share information about support resources with the team
opts.rat: Recognizing caregiver strain and connecting the family with support resources (e.g., respite, support groups) is part of holistic dementia care.
